serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fish salad with four toppings and no sauce, approximately 300 g, as typically served at a salad bar or casual dining establishment in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Salad (4 toppings), no sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Fish salad (4 toppings), no sauce' (cr_id F08010023). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogous evidence: mixed green salad with protein has a very low GI due to dominant non-starchy vegetable and lean protein composition. Reference: GI of mixed salad with protein components is consistently reported in the low range (GI 20–40) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 2.94 g/100 g = 8.82 g); GL = (32 × 8.82) / 100 = 2.8 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 317 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 951 mg sodium, representing about 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request that fish be prepared without added salt and confirm that no high-sodium pickled or brined toppings are included, as the sauce-free preparation already removes the primary sodium contributor but the fish itself may be seasoned.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.94 g/100 g), low sugar (2.41 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (2.24 g/100 g), and good protein (4.55 g/100 g) produce a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 32 and a glycaemic load of only 2.8 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well suited for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; patients should ensure fish is grilled or baked rather than battered, and moderate starchy toppings such as corn or beetroot to limit glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.88 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (2.24 g/100 g), and lean protein from fish support a heart-healthy dietary pattern; omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish varieties provide additional cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose oily fish varieties such as salmon, mackerel, or sardine to maximise omega-3 intake, avoid high-sodium toppings, and use lemon juice or herbs in place of sauce to maintain a low saturated fat and low sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Four diverse vegetable toppings provide antioxidants; fish provides protein and potential omega-3 fatty acids; absence of sauce and very low total fat support an anti-inflammatory dietary profile.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should select oily fish for maximum omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit and avoid nightshade toppings (tomato, bell pepper, eggplant) if solanine sensitivity is a concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Salad with Four Toppings (No Sauce) is a freshly assembled, minimally processed salad dish consisting of a portion of cooked fish — typically grilled, baked, or pan-seared — served over a bed of fresh mixed greens and accompanied by a customer's choice of four vegetable or salad toppings. The dish is served without any sauce or dressing, making it one of the lowest-calorie and lowest-sodium options in the customisable salad category. Per 100 g, the dish provides 47 kcal, 4.55 g protein, 1.88 g fat, 2.94 g carbohydrate, 2.41 g sugar, 2.24 g dietary fibre, and 317 mg sodium, with 31 mg calcium and 0.68 mg iron also reported. The texture is a combination of tender, flaky fish and crisp raw greens, with flavour depending on the fish species and toppings selected. Clinically, this dish is well suited for type 2 diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate content, low glycaemic index (estimated GI ~32), and high fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio. The sauce-free preparation reduces sodium load significantly compared to dressed salad variants, making it a reasonable choice for hypertension management when fish is prepared without added salt. The lean protein and low saturated fat profile support cardiovascular health, particularly when oily fish such as salmon or mackerel is selected for its omega-3 fatty acid content. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible. It is commonly found at salad bars and casual dining establishments in Singapore and is classified under customisable mixed dishes in the HPB Singapore food composition database (cr_id F08010023).
common_user_queries: Is fish salad good for diabetes?
Is fish salad good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010023) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food composition data. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from analogous evidence in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this customisable composite dish as of 2026. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 4.55, fat_g 1.88, carbohydrate_g 2.94) are non-zero. This is a Western-cuisine dish with no mandatory local-language naming requirements under the Veritas multilingual rules; local_names fields are left null accordingly. GI of approximately 32 is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI profile of mixed green salads with lean protein as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for this specific composite dish was found in approved sources. Serving size of 300 g is an estimated standard plate size for a customisable salad dish; no HPB-specified portion size was available in the input record.
